extra.message:
Client-Side Authorization Control Bypass

The courses template checks auth_user.is_admin to conditionally display the 'Add new course' form, but the backend view (sqli/views.py) does not enforce this authorization check. Attackers can bypass the UI restriction and send direct POST requests to create courses without admin privileges.

Impact: Non-admin users can create courses and modify application content by bypassing client-side controls

Fix: Implement server-side authorization checks in the view functions using the @authorize(ensure_admin=True) decorator, rather than relying on client-side UI hiding.

CSRF Protection Middleware Disabled

The CSRF middleware is commented out in the Application initialization, leaving all state-changing POST operations (login, logout, student creation, course creation, reviews, evaluations) vulnerable to Cross-Site Request Forgery attacks.

Fix: Uncomment csrf_middleware to enable CSRF token validation on POST requests.

Global XSS due to Disabled Jinja2 Autoescape

The Jinja2 template engine is initialized with autoescape=False, globally disabling automatic HTML entity encoding for all template variables. This causes every `{{ variable }}` expression throughout the application to render raw HTML/JavaScript if the variable contains user-controlled data, enabling both reflected and stored XSS attacks across the entire application surface.

Impact: Attackers can inject and execute arbitrary JavaScript in victims' browsers, leading to session hijacking, credential theft, and unauthorized actions on behalf of users.

Fix: Enable autoescape by setting autoescape=True (the default), or if disabling is absolutely necessary for specific templates, use the |e filter to explicitly escape all user-controlled output.

SQL Injection via String Interpolation in Student Creation

The Student.create() method uses Python string formatting ('%(name)s' % {'name': name}) to construct SQL queries. This interpolates user-controlled input directly into the SQL string before passing it to the database cursor, creating a classic SQL injection vulnerability. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL commands via the name parameter (e.g., "test'); DROP TABLE students; --" or "test') UNION SELECT * FROM users--").

Impact: Attackers can execute arbitrary SQL commands to read, modify, or delete database contents, bypass authentication, or execute administrative operations on the database server.

Fix: Use proper parameterized queries by passing parameters as the second argument to execute(): await cur.execute("INSERT INTO students (name) VALUES (%(name)s)", {'name': name}). Never use Python string formatting (% operator) or f-strings for SQL queries.

Session Cookies Missing HttpOnly Flag

Session cookies are created with httponly=False, allowing client-side JavaScript to access the session ID via document.cookie. This enables session hijacking attacks when combined with XSS vulnerabilities (which are trivially exploitable due to autoescape=False).

Fix: Set httponly=True to prevent JavaScript access to session cookies.

Stored XSS in User Profile Navigation

Authenticated user first_name, middle_name, and last_name are rendered in the navigation bar without escaping. If user profile data is attacker-controlled (via user registration/update features), stored XSS payloads execute on every page load for authenticated users.

Impact: Session hijacking via XSS in the navigation bar that appears on every authenticated page view.

Fix: Apply the |e filter to all user name fields: `{{ auth_user.first_name | e }}`.

Stored XSS in Course Review Display

User-supplied review text is retrieved from the database and rendered without HTML escaping. Since the review creation endpoint (views.py:129) stores raw user input without sanitization, and autoescape is disabled, malicious JavaScript payloads submitted as reviews will execute in the browsers of all users viewing the course.

Impact: Persistent XSS allows attackers to store JavaScript payloads that execute for every user viewing the course, enabling mass session hijacking and credential theft.

Fix: Apply the |e filter: `{{ review.review_text | e }}`, or preferably enable global autoescape.

Stored XSS in Course Review Display

User-supplied review text is retrieved from the database and rendered without HTML escaping. Since the review creation endpoint (views.py:129) stores raw user input without sanitization, and autoescape is disabled, malicious JavaScript payloads submitted as reviews will execute in the browsers of all users viewing the course.

Impact: Persistent XSS allows attackers to store JavaScript payloads that execute for every user viewing the course, enabling mass session hijacking and credential theft.

Fix: Apply the |e filter: `{{ review.review_text | e }}`, or preferably enable global autoescape.

Stored XSS in Course List Display

Course titles and descriptions are rendered in a list view without HTML escaping. User-controlled course data stored in the database is output directly into the HTML response.

Impact: JavaScript payloads in course listings execute for all users browsing the course catalog.

Fix: Apply the |e filter: `{{ course.title | e }}` and `{{ course.description | e }}`.

Stored XSS in Evaluation Error Pages

Student and course names are rendered in the evaluation error template without escaping. While line 9 explicitly uses `| e`, lines 25 and 28 omit the escape filter, rendering raw HTML from database entries.

Impact: XSS execution when evaluation validation fails and the error page is displayed.

Fix: Add |e filter: `{{ student.name | e }}` and `{{ course.name | e }}`.

Stored XSS in Review Page Title

Course title is rendered in the review form page without escaping on line 32, despite being escaped in the breadcrumb on line 10.

Impact: XSS execution when viewing the review submission page for a course with a malicious title.

Fix: Add |e filter: `{{ course.title | e }}`.

Stored XSS in Student Detail Course List

While student names are escaped on lines 3, 9, and 14, course titles and descriptions rendered within the student detail page (lines 19-20) are not escaped, allowing stored XSS via course data.

Impact: JavaScript execution via malicious course data when viewing student records.

Fix: Add |e filter to course outputs: `{{ course.title | e }}` and `{{ course.description | e }}`.

Stored XSS in Student Names

Student names are user-controlled inputs (created via views.py:57) and rendered without escaping in the student list. Malicious names stored in the database execute as JavaScript when the student list is viewed.

Impact: Attackers can create students with XSS payloads in their names, affecting all users viewing the student directory.

Fix: Apply the |e filter: `{{ name | e }}`, or enable global autoescape.

Missing Authentication on Review Creation

The POST handler for /courses/{course_id}/review allows anonymous users to submit reviews. There is no authentication check, allowing anyone to spam reviews or inject malicious content (XSS payloads) that will be stored and displayed to other users.

Impact: Unauthorized review posting; stored XSS via review_text; spam/reputation attacks

Fix: Add authentication requirement: check for auth_user before processing POST data, or use @authorize() decorator.

Hardcoded Database Credentials in Configuration

The database configuration file contains hardcoded default credentials (postgres/postgres). These weak credentials may persist in production deployments if configuration management practices are inadequate. Hardcoded credentials in source code or configuration files expose the database to unauthorized access if the repository or configuration files are compromised.

Fix: Remove hardcoded credentials from configuration files. Use environment variables or a secure secrets management system (like Docker Secrets, Kubernetes Secrets, or HashiCorp Vault) to inject credentials at runtime. Change default passwords before deployment.

Debug Mode Enabled in Production

The aiohttp Application is initialized with debug=True, enabling detailed error pages with full stack traces. This exposes internal implementation details, file paths, and potentially sensitive configuration information to attackers. Combined with the 50x.jinja2 template that renders error.__dict__, this leaks significant internal application state.

Fix: Set debug=False in production environments. Use environment variables to control debug mode: debug=os.environ.get('DEBUG', 'false').lower() == 'true'.

Weak Password Hashing Using MD5

Password verification uses MD5 hashing without salt. MD5 is cryptographically broken, extremely fast (facilitating brute-force attacks), and vulnerable to rainbow table attacks. The absence of salting means identical passwords produce identical hashes, allowing attackers to pre-compute hash tables. Given the PII context, compromised passwords could expose user identities across the application and other services where users reuse credentials.

Fix: Replace MD5 with a modern password hashing algorithm designed for passwords such as bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2. Use a library like 'bcrypt' or 'argon2-cffi'. Example: bcrypt.checkpw(password.encode('utf-8'), self.pwd_hash.encode('utf-8')). Ensure passwords are hashed with salt during user creation/registration as well.

Weak Password Hashing Using MD5

Password verification uses MD5 hashing without salt. MD5 is cryptographically broken, extremely fast (facilitating brute-force attacks), and vulnerable to rainbow table attacks. The absence of salting means identical passwords produce identical hashes, allowing attackers to pre-compute hash tables. Given the PII context, compromised passwords could expose user identities across the application and other services where users reuse credentials.

Fix: Replace MD5 with a modern password hashing algorithm designed for passwords such as bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2. Use a library like 'bcrypt' or 'argon2-cffi'. Example: bcrypt.checkpw(password.encode('utf-8'), self.pwd_hash.encode('utf-8')). Ensure passwords are hashed with salt during user creation/registration as well.

Outdated jQuery 3.2.1 with Multiple Critical CVEs

Bundled jQuery version 3.2.1 (March 2017) contains multiple known critical vulnerabilities. CVE-2019-11358 allows prototype pollution via jQuery.extend(true, {}, ...) enabling attackers to modify Object.prototype and affect application-wide behavior. CVE-2020-11022 and CVE-2020-11023 allow XSS attacks when passing HTML containing <option> or <style> elements to DOM manipulation methods like .html() or .append(). CVE-2015-9251 permits XSS via cross-domain AJAX requests when the response is interpreted as JavaScript.

Impact: Attackers can execute arbitrary JavaScript in user browsers, pollute JavaScript prototypes to alter application logic, or bypass security controls via script injection through jQuery's HTML manipulation methods.

Fix: Upgrade jQuery to version 3.6.0 or later (preferably 3.7.1+) to mitigate all known CVEs. If immediate upgrade is not possible, implement strict input validation and avoid passing user-controlled data to jQuery HTML manipulation methods.

Outdated Materialize CSS Framework with Known XSS Vulnerabilities

Materialize v0.100.1 (2017) is an outdated frontend framework containing multiple unpatched XSS vulnerabilities. The framework uses unsafe DOM methods (innerHTML, .html()) without proper sanitization in components activated by init.js. This version predates security fixes for XSS in tooltip, modal, and toast components.

Impact: Cross-site scripting attacks via Materialize components can lead to session hijacking, credential theft, and malicious action execution under user privileges. Combined with the application's disabled template autoescaping, this creates a high-risk attack surface.

Fix: Upgrade Materialize to version 1.0.0 or later, or migrate to a maintained alternative. If upgrading is not feasible, sanitize all user inputs before passing to Materialize components and disable HTML content in tooltips.

Missing Authorization on Student Evaluation

The evaluate() view lacks any authorization checks, allowing any user to submit grades for any student in any course. There is no verification that the user is authenticated or has administrative privileges before creating Mark records.

Fix: Add @authorize(ensure_admin=True) decorator to the evaluate() view to restrict grade creation to administrators only.

Excessive Service Exposure (Database Port)

The docker-compose configuration exposes the PostgreSQL database port (5432) to the host network (0.0.0.0:5432). This is unnecessary for internal service communication within Docker networks and increases the attack surface by allowing external connections to the database.

Impact: External attackers can attempt to connect to the database directly. Combined with hardcoded/default credentials, this grants immediate unauthorized database access bypassing application security controls.

Fix: Remove the ports mapping for the postgres service unless external database access is specifically required. Docker services can communicate internally using service names (e.g., 'postgres:5432') without exposing ports to the host. If external access is needed, restrict it to localhost (127.0.0.1:5432:5432) or implement proper firewall rules.

Sensitive Information Disclosure via Error Templates

The error template for 50x status codes renders the entire error object dictionary (error.__dict__), potentially exposing internal application state, configuration details, file paths, or database connection information when exceptions occur.

Fix: Remove the error.__dict__ output from error templates. Display only generic, user-friendly error messages in production. Log detailed errors securely server-side instead.
